steps.text: Conduct testing to ensure that sampling techniques written for a specific organism are effective for additional organisms.
Conduct validation to ensure that techniques tested for collecting samples for one type of assay will perform with equal proficiency with the proposed assays.
Address the precise procedures of a sampling technique.
Indicate all physical sampling techniques or collection tools that are used.
Indicate whether the procedure is a field measurement process such as screening or a direct reading.
Optimize each significant parameter serially.
Test previously optimized parameters to ensure that they remain optimal.
Make necessary adjustments to parameters.
Modify, amend, or otherwise annotate the technique when parameter discontinuities are revealed over time.
Include known operational limits in documentation.
Describe testing conditions so that potential users can assess whether test conditions accurately replicate potential difficulties.

Incorporate all technique performance components, including personnel, laboratory facilities, reagents, supplies, equipment, calibrations, reporting standards, record keeping, data analysis, safety and quality.

Evaluate the selected sampling technique based on project goals, matrix, location of microbes, heterogeneity, sampling device, the target itself, exposure to sunlight, and introduction of dissolved oxygen.
Select surrogates ensuring that the properties of the control(s) are similar to those of the target organisms, especially regarding interaction with the matrix and sampling device.
Conduct sampling and downstream processing at lower temperatures (4°C) within hours to days.
Document required sampling measurements and matrix parameters (such as date, location, time, weather, etc.).
Assess the potential impact of sample collection and transportation on the parameter of interest.
Measure background levels of the microbe of interest using an unspiked matrix blank.
Subtract the background levels from the recovery calculations.
Run unspiked blank samples each time a sampling procedure is completed.
Discard or repeat the run.
Acquire duplicate or replicate samples whenever feasible.
Run spiked samples or positive controls for each sampling procedure.
Repeat the technique or discard/invalidate the results.
Take trip or travel blanks from the laboratory to the sampling site and return them unopened.
Complete field sampling forms at the time of sampling.
Perform field measurements (such as pH and temperature) on a separate subsample.
Discard the separate subsample used for field measurements.
Classify collected samples and generated wastes as either environmental or hazardous materials (dangerous goods) samples prior to shipment.
Package, label, and ship potentially hazardous materials according to the appropriate ground or air regulations.
Store samples or wastes in a secure area that is protected from vermin and adverse weather conditions.
Separate hazardous and nonhazardous samples and wastes.
Prepare a suitable sampling technique validation report for placing in the public docket.
Address the sampling technique validation topics, including background, description, validation practices, changes, and recommendations.
Provide the SOP or a thorough description of the sampling technique that is being validated.
Provide an overall summary of the validation report.
Provide background information on the sampling technique development.
State the purpose of the technique, including the measurement objectives and the intended use of the data.
Describe how selectivity was evaluated and how the sampling plan identified and addressed interferences.
Describe the sensitivity of the technique and how the sampling plan addressed it.
Describe how specificity was evaluated and how the sampling plan addressed it.

Describe how the sampling plan addresses viability assays, if required/applicable, and what the requirements are for maintaining/preserving viability. Describe sample security from the collection site to the analytical laboratory.
Discuss the number of items or the quantity that constitutes an adequate sample for the technique, and whether the samples are composites or grab.
Describe how the sampling technique matches the performance of the applicable analytical assay technique(s).
Describe sampling technique performance after experiencing minor changes in operating or environmental conditions.
Describe the applicable resource requirements, such as personnel, skills, and equipment, as well as the required measurements to be taken, such as site parameters and matrix parameters.
Describe safety concerns and procedures that should be addressed, including personal protective equipment and first aid as well as physical and chemical hazards.
Describe the QC/QA checks used.
Discuss technique development.
Discuss validation testing results. Evaluate the testing, including comparison with reference materials and preparations, acceptance criteria and recommendations.
Discuss sampling technique changes that were made as a result of the validation studies.
Discuss formal acceptance/rejection of work.
Provide recommendations for future work.
Peer review EPA sampling techniques according to the current version of the EPA Science Policy Council’s Peer Review Handbook.

preconditions.text: Prior to using sampling techniques written for a specific organism on additional organisms (even when the microbes are closely related).
Prior to using techniques tested for collecting samples for one type of assay with proposed assays.
Developing or validating sampling tools and techniques
Consider the ability to capture the surrogate in a manner similar to the target when selecting a sampling technique.
If viability measurements are included or if viable organisms are to be detected
If a split or replicate of a sample is to be sent to a laboratory for analysis
If the blanks are positive for the selected microorganism
If positive controls are not positive for the microorganism of interest
Classification must be performed by certified DOT/IATA personnel.
Personnel performing shipments of potentially hazardous materials must have approved DOT/IATA training.
The sampling technique must be validated appropriately and verified by field studies.
Prior to publication of EPA sampling techniques

constraints.text: Optimization should be weighted toward parameters that represent significant programmatic concerns rather than those that are easiest to measure.
Performance measures of sampling techniques cannot be measured in the absence of an appropriate analytical assay or set of assays.
Performance measures cannot be interpreted in the absence of specific combinations of matrix analyte, sampling technique, analytical assay and other processing or analytical procedures.
Air incorporated into a water sample during collection may change the pH of the sample from an anoxic environment.
Field measurements must be performed on a separate subsample to avoid contaminating samples for laboratory analysis.
A conductivity measurement should not be prepared with a sample that previously was used to measure pH, as potassium chloride from the pH probe may affect the conductivity reading.
Samples and wastes may be subject to DOT hazardous material regulations (HMR) and the CDC’s Select Agent Program requirements.
Air shipments must comply with IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations.
Suspected contaminated samples or wastes must be shipped as dangerous goods or possibly as select agents.
Sample and packaging integrity must be maintained to ensure safe shipment.
At a minimum, the sampling technique validation report must address the information contained in Table 1.
